items.title: Hepatic sporozoite cycle
items.contentHtml:
Hepatic exoerythrocytic cycle

  • The sporozoites inoculated by the Anopheles mosquito migrate to the liver in minutes and invade hepatocytes.
  • In the hepatocyte they mature into a hepatic schizont in 5–16 days, releasing thousands of merozoites into the bloodstream. blood.
  • P. vivax and P. ovale form hypnozoites (latent hepatic forms) that cause relapses months or years later.
  • The hepatic phase is asymptomatic and does not produce detectable parasitaemia in peripheral blood.
  • Primaquine is the only drug capable of eliminating hypnozoites, requiring screening for G6PD deficiency prior to its administration. use.

items.title: Cerebral malaria due to P. falciparum
items.contentHtml:
Cerebral malaria

  • Produced by P. falciparum due to cytoadhesion of parasitized erythrocytes to the cerebral endothelium, mediated by PfEMP1.
  • Diagnostic criteria: Glasgow <11 or Blantyre <3 in children, with P. falciparum in the blood and exclusion of other causes of encephalopathy.
  • Mortality of 15–20% even with treatment; neurological sequelae (epilepsy, motor deficit) in 10–20% of survivors.
  • First-line treatment: intravenous artesunate (2.4 mg/kg at 0, 12 and 24 hours, then daily).
  • Do not use corticosteroids or heparin; hypoglycemia is a common complication and should be monitored.

items.foco: Tertian fever due to P. vivax and P. ovale
items.title: Benign tertian fever
items.contentHtml:
Tertian fever

  • Classic febrile pattern: fever peaks every 48 hours (tertian), corresponding to the synchronous rupture of erythrocytic schizonts.
  • Produced by P. vivax and P. oval; quartan fever (every 72 hours) is typical ofP. malariae.
  • The fever is not continuous; between attacks the patient may feel relatively well.
  • The differential diagnosis includes dengue, leptospirosis and typhoid fever; the thick smear confirms the parasite.
  • Radical treatment requires chloroquine (if sensitive) plus primaquine to eliminate hypnozoites and prevent relapses.

items.foco: Hemolytic anemia due to rupture of erythrocytes
items.title: Hemolytic anemia in malaria
items.contentHtml:
Hemolytic anemia

  • Mechanism: direct rupture of erythrocytes by mature schizonts (intravascular hemolysis) plus ineffective erythropoiesis and splenic sequestration.
  • The anemia is normocytic normochromic; in chronic malaria there may be hyperreactive splenomegaly.
  • In P. falciparum anemia is more severe due to greater parasitemia and destruction of non-parasitized erythrocytes by immune mechanisms.
  • hemoglobin <7 g/dL in severe malaria is a criterion for blood transfusion.
  • Massive hemolysis can cause hemoglobinuria (blackwater fever), associated with G6PD deficiency and use of quinine.

items.foco: Hypoglycemia as a complication of treatment
items.title: Quinine/artesunate-induced hypoglycemia
items.contentHtml:
Hypoglycemia in malaria

  • Frequent complication in severe malaria due to P. falciparum, especially in children and pregnant women.
  • Mechanism: hyperinsulinemia induced by quinine (stimulates insulin release) and glucose consumption by parasites.
  • Hypoglycemia (<40 mg/dL) can be asymptomatic or manifest with seizures, coma or neurological deterioration.
  • Artesunate has less risk of hypoglycemia than quinine, but still requires glycemic monitoring every 4–6 h.
  • Treatment: 50% intravenous dextrose (1 mL/kg) or intramuscular glucagon; if it persists, continuous infusion of 10% glucose.

items.title: Artemisinin combination therapy (ACT)
items.contentHtml:
Artemisinin-lumefantrine

  • First line for malaria not complicated by P. falciparum in areas of resistance to chloroquine.
  • Presentation: tablets of 20 mg of artemether + 120 mg of lumefantrine; dose according to weight (1 tablet/5 kg, twice a day for 3 days).
  • artemisinin quickly reduces parasitemia (action on annular stages and trophozoites); lumefantrine eliminates residual parasites.
  • It should be administered with fatty foods to improve absorption of lumefantrine.
  • Contraindicated in first trimester pregnancy (category C); use quinine + clindamycin as an alternative.

items.foco: Diagnosis by thick smear and blood smear
items.title: Thick smear and smear in malaria
items.contentHtml:
Microscopic diagnosis

  • Thick smear: concentrates parasites (10–20 times more sensitive than smear), allows quantification of parasitemia (parasites/μL). Detection threshold: ~5–10 parasites/μL.
  • Blood smear (thin smear): identifies species and stage (ring, trophozoite, schizont, gametocyte); essential to differentiateP. falciparum from other species.
  • In P. falciparumonly rings and gametocytes are seen in peripheral blood; schizonts are sequestered in deep organs.
  • Parasitaemia >5% in P. falciparum defines severe malaria.
  • Rapid antigen tests (RDT) detect HRP2 and pLDH, useful in the field.

items.title: Antimalarial chemoprophylaxis
items.contentHtml:
Pharmacological prophylaxis

  • Indicated for travelers to endemic areas with resistance to chloroquine.
  • Mefloquine: 250 mg/week, start 2–3 weeks before, continue 4 weeks after departure. Contraindicated in psychiatric disorders or epilepsy.
  • Doxycycline: 100 mg/day, start 1–2 days before, continue 4 weeks after release. Avoid in children <8 years and pregnant women.
  • Atovaquone-proguanil (Malarone): 1 tablet/day, start 1–2 days before, continue 7 days after departure. Better tolerated but more expensive.
  • No prophylaxis is 100% effective; It must be complemented with barrier measures (repellent, mosquito net).

items.title: Hyperreactive splenomegaly
items.contentHtml:
Hyperreactive splenomegaly (tropical splenomegaly syndrome)

  • Associated with chronic infection by P. malariaeor P. vivax, with hyperproduction of IgM and formation of immune complexes.
  • Clinic: massive splenomegaly (>10 cm below the costal margin), abdominal pain, anemia, leukopenia and thrombocytopenia.
  • Diagnostic criteria: palpable splenomegaly, high titers of antimalarial antibodies, improvement with antimalarial prophylaxis prolonged.
  • Treatment: prophylaxis with chloroquine or sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine for 6–12 months; If there is no response, consider splenectomy.
  • Risk of spontaneous splenic rupture and infections due to encapsulated germs.

items.title: Chloroquine resistance
items.contentHtml:
Chloroquine resistance

  • Widespread globally in P. falciparum, mediated by mutations in the pfcrt gene (chloroquine resistance transporter).
  • Chloroquine is no longer used as a first line for P. falciparumin most regions; except in Central America and the Caribbean.
  • In P. vivaxchloroquine resistance is emerging (reported in Indonesia, Papua New Guinea).
  • Susceptibility test: parasitaemia monitoring at 48–72 h; if it does not decrease >90%, suspect resistance.
  • Alternatives: artemisinin-lumefantrine, quinine + doxycycline or atovaquone-proguanil.

items.foco: Primaquina eliminates hypnozoites in P. vivax
items.title: Primaquine for P. vivax radical
items.contentHtml:
Primaquine and hypnozoites

  • Only drug active against hepatic hypnozoites of P. vivax and P. ovale, preventing relapses.
  • Dose: 0.5 mg/kg/day (base) for 14 days; in resistance areas 0.5 mg/kg/day is used for 14 days or a total dose of 3.5 mg/kg.
  • Contraindicated in G6PD deficiency (risk of severe hemolysis); Prior screening must be performed.
  • In mild G6PD deficiency, primaquine can be used weekly (0.75 mg/kg/week for 8 weeks) with monitoring.
  • It should not be administered during pregnancy or lactation; In these cases, use chloroquine weekly as a suppressant until delivery.

items.title: Malaria transmitted by transfusion
items.contentHtml:
Post-transfusion malaria

  • Transmission by asymptomatic donor with low parasitemia; P. malariae can persist for years in blood without symptoms.
  • The parasite survives in stored blood for up to 1–2 weeks (depending on species and anticoagulant).
  • Clinical: fever, anemia, splenomegaly days to weeks after transfusion; there is no hepatic phase (absence of sporozoites).
  • Prevention: defer donors with a history of malaria (1 year after returning from an endemic area, 3 years if they had malaria).
  • In endemic areas, donor screening is performed with thick smears or rapid tests; There is no approved universal screening test.
